note:
从诺瓦鲁登陆Twitch的那一刻起，她的某些东西看起来就像是美元符号。当时19岁的诺瓦鲁金发碧眼，脸上挂着灿烂的笑容，她和越来越多的粉丝一起玩游戏和闲逛只有几周的时间，就开始收到创业观众的信息，提出要得到她的“交易”-含糊的承诺将她与一个资本b品牌联系起来。
其中一个特别突出。他提供管理技巧-玩什么游戏，怎么玩-并说他会让她的免费产品在流媒体上做广告。然而，过了一段时间，诺瓦鲁察觉到这个人，用她的话说，是“可疑的”。他的管理技巧主要包括告诉她玩游戏，效仿他喜欢的其他流媒体。他也没有给她带来赞助--只是产品折扣的会员链接，任何人只要有网络浏览器和Twitch个人资料中的免费空间就可以获得。他似乎终究没有这方面的背景，幸运的是，这也意味着他从未让她签署过正式合同。诺瓦鲁把他扔下了。几个月后，她了解到，在更改用户名后，他也与另一家流媒体公司接洽，希望管理这些网站。
诺瓦鲁对这种不专业的态度感到沮丧，他接受了一家专门处理职业游戏玩家的老牌管理公司的采访。这些公司是与蓬勃发展的直播游戏行业同步出现的，根据流媒体软件公司Streamlabs的数据，该行业目前价值500亿美元。有了数以万计的追随者，诺瓦鲁决定是时候把她的竞选正规化了。尖顶管理公司拥有一切：豪华的网站，一个适合国际航空公司的标志，以及一份实际的合同。尽管斯皮尔从Twitch上赚取了7%的收入，以及他们为她获得的15%的赞助，但它们让诺瓦鲁感觉自己像个职业选手。但在经历了一些早期的拉动之后，赞助交易枯竭了。
诺瓦鲁说：“我每个月自掏腰包付给斯皮尔100到130美元，甚至每个月都得不到一笔品牌交易。”“我对此感到不舒服。”(SPIRE没有回应《连线》的置评请求。)。
Twitch每月有620万名广播员。其中4.5万的人拥有“合作伙伴”身份，这意味着他们可以通过捐款和广告将自己的流媒体货币化。只有几百人在站台外过着不错的全职生活，扮演堡垒之夜、英雄联盟或使命召唤现场直播，而旁观者则充斥着隔壁聊天室的表情。这并没有阻止无数有抱负的专业人士试图进入这一上层梯队。
随着像泰勒·“忍者”布莱文斯这样的摇滚明星流媒体获得六七位数的赞助，从艾伦到时代广场的广告牌无处不在，管理游戏名人的业务呈爆炸式增长。在过去的六年里，数十家机构或管理公司突然出现，专门为Twitch、Facebook Gaming和YouTube的游戏频道的微名人提供服务。质量代表可能意味着与G Fuel、红牛、罗技(Logitech)、联想(Lenovo)等公司，甚至是MAC或Postmate等非本土品牌达成有利可图的交易。它可以提供支持，比如整理流媒体用户的电子邮件，分享社交媒体诀窍，追究他们对交付成果的责任，以及帮助预订在大会上露面。
然而，管理流媒体的业务与更广泛的行业一样，是一个狂野的西部。随着CAA、WME和United Talent等传统人才经纪公司进入这一领域，名人游戏玩家逐渐意识到，他们习惯的不驯服的环境并不是常态。
在对目前或以前与流媒体管理行业有关联的20多人的采访中，许多人描述了他们所见过或经历过的猖獗的剥削。消息人士详细介绍了管理公司从交易顶部掠夺额外资金，充当无照代理，不提供退出条款，有时还会在幕后收到回扣的提议。正如在适用的情况下所指出的那样，几位为这篇报道接受有线采访的消息人士后来也成为了性行为不当指控的对象，这是6月份游戏#MeToo运动的一部分。“连线”还审查了该领域一些最大公司的六份合同，以证实消息来源的说法。
“目前还没有每个人都制定或认可的行业标准。End Game Talent总裁、Twitch前员工斯科特·鲍尔(Scott Ball)表示：“当没有行业规范标准时，人们可以尝试做任何他们想做的事情。”
年轻的、目中无人的孩子，他们的第一份工作是在一个不欠他们任何东西的平台上直播视频游戏：谁会更容易成为目标？
经理人最初是通过YouTube进入游戏娱乐行业的。一旦一些早期的YouTuber获得了微名人地位，多频道网络，如Maker Studios-成立于2009年，并于2014年被迪士尼收购-和Machinima承诺帮助创作者依靠不断变化的平台及其不断变化的政策稳定地生活。没过多久，这些业务就变得糟糕起来；许多早期的YouTube创建者依赖于非物质的“观点”和不可靠的广告销售，得到的承诺比他们收到的要多。2017年，Maker放弃了对近6万名YouTube的支持；他们中的许多人感到松了一口气。几位YouTube用户陷入了无休止的自动续签合同，有时还牺牲了25%的收入，他们抱怨Maker的成本超过了它的贡献-至少如果你还不是超级巨星的话。
到2011年，新生的流媒体网站Justin.tv凭借游戏获得了如此大的吸引力，以至于它推出了Twitch来满足观众对直播内容的胃口。技术娴熟、富有魅力的游戏玩家现在有了自己的平台，可以让自己脱颖而出，成为明星。随着Twitch的成长，星际争霸II中的明星、饰演英雄联盟、暗黑三世最大暗黑破坏神和使命召唤兄弟的辣妹们都增加了观众，并成为了一条潜在的职业道路-或者至少是一条兼职-玩视频游戏。与此同时，体育专业人士、锦标赛组织者和游戏发行商正在为这场据报道将成为今天价值10亿美元的体育产业的大火而聚集在一起。Twitch流媒体和ESPORTS的承诺加在一起，将慢慢使职业游戏正常化-在过去，这是孩子们的白日梦。
老牌的管理机构在转向Twitch方面行动迟缓，这一空白主要由业余人士和版主填补，他们承诺提供不切实际的赞助或提前获得低预算的独立游戏。更上一层楼的是那些在AAA开发商或出版商有真正关系的朋友的朋友。
2015年，乔希·贝尔金(Josh Belkin)在游戏工作室Hi-rez从事ESPORTS编程时，有一个两面派的想法：他的一个朋友在推出游戏“最黑暗的地下城”；他的其他几个朋友在Twitch上播放游戏。“我给朋友发短信说，‘嘿，我能帮你推销你的游戏发布吗？’”流媒体用户从玩新游戏的新颖性中既赚到了钱，又获得了影响力。开发商从有机的有影响力的营销中受益。在一开始免费工作之后，贝尔金开始要求他给流媒体带来的有偿活动的15%。
“这只是口口相传，”贝尔金说。“我们带来了一些付费激活的流媒体，因为我有一些业内的朋友，他们的预算太多了。”贝尔金现在是一家为流媒体开发软件的公司的首席执行官，他向客户提供商业方向、他们一周的内容、如何扩大流媒体，以及如何在社交渠道上吸引观众的建议--这些事情他当时只是有点，有点知道怎么做。
其他年轻的绿色Twitch彩带粉丝也开始流行起来。贾斯汀·沃登(Justin Warden)现在是管理公司和营销机构Ader的首席执行官，当他22岁住在朋友的沙发上时，他开始怀疑，有影响力的营销是否是应对广告拦截软件破坏性影响的一种潜在的有利可图的解决办法。典狱长获得了赞助，参加了第一届Twitch Con，这是一场为Twitch Streamers和他们的粉丝举办的大型大会。在一件粉红色的沃尔玛T恤背面，他用夏皮写道：“问我们是否想要赞助。”
典狱长刚刚从大学辍学，没有任何管理背景。一年后，他在拜伦·伯恩斯坦(Byron“Reckful”Bernstein)的聊天中发送了垃圾邮件表情，直到他引起了这位受欢迎的流媒体用户的注意。在伯恩斯坦制作的一段视频中，他解释了在他们认识之前，沃登只是另一个在他的聊天中发布表情的人，他的视频名为“Reckful意外地帮助一个随机的观众建立了一家价值数百万美元的公司”。但有一天，沃登决定给赛博游戏公司的一家子公司发电子邮件，询问他们是否愿意付钱给伯恩斯坦来玩“影子世界”。当他们同意的时候，典狱长给伯恩斯坦发了一封冷冰冰的电子邮件，说他想要分成。啊，真灵。然后，监狱长对另一条彩带和另一条彩带使用了同样的策略。(伯恩斯坦于7月去世。)。
他在早期尝试的基础上建立的公司Ader，现在管理着数十个流媒体，拥有20名员工。在2018年Reddit上一篇现已删除的帖子中，题为“我如何在没有经验的情况下，在两年内将Ader的年收入从0美元提高到800万美元”，他的要点之一包括“过度承诺和过度交付”。
布兰登·弗莱塔格(Brandon Freytag)是代表超级明星流媒体的顶级管理公司Load的创始人兼人才高级副总裁。2016年，当他在Twitch抢到了第一批客户时，他正在从事体育营销工作：SaQib“LIRIK”Zahid和Jaryd“Summit1g”Lazar。他说，他们没有可用于接收业务的电子邮件，所以Freytag在一笔5美元的捐款中附上了一条信息来自我介绍。(今天，扎希德有260万粉丝，拉扎尔有530万。Load还管理着泰勒·“忍者”布莱文斯(Tyler“Ninja”Blevins)。)。
在最初的几年里，流媒体一直低估了自己的价值。由于没有讨价还价的商业诀窍，游戏内容创建者往往会低调或基本上要求随机数字，他们的朋友之友经理也是如此。电子竞技尤其糟糕。当他刚刚开始的时候，长期从事“使命召唤”的职业球员詹姆斯·克莱斯特·尤班克斯说，他对成为一支好球队的热情超过了他对合同的怀疑。(团队分担一些与经理相同的职责。)。
“这是一个机会，”他说。“他们不会给我们更优惠的条件。就好像他们控制了一切。我们只是签了摆在我们面前的任何东西。“。他说，当他与特工打交道时，情况也好不到哪里去。他说，在体育领域与他合作的绝大多数经纪公司都没有像他们花费的那样赚到那么多钱。他说：“我和几家中介公司打交道，他们都索要毛收入的5%到30%。”“他们给我带来了几笔品牌交易，差一点就扯平了。但最终，我几乎总是付给经纪人比他们给我更多的钱。“。
“这只是不理解杠杆，”下一代电子竞技的高级客户主管本·尼科尔(Ben Nicholl)说。“当你20岁的时候，正试图为你的朋友洽谈你的第一份六位数的合同，在两周等待期的第三天，你可能会给他回电话说，‘去他妈的，我们接受它！’”
电子竞技专业人士和Twitch流媒体并不一定是瑜伽色的，也不一定是晒得黝黑的，但到了中期，AAA游戏发行商和开发商已经认识到，对于拥有大量可支配收入的人群来说，游戏明星是有影响力的-真正的、可联系的人。管理Twitch拖网很快就会从一项副业正式成为一份工作。
2012年加入SOE的奥米德·达里亚尼(Omeed Dariani)表示：“我在索尼在线娱乐公司(Sony Online Entertainment)工作的第一天，我的老板约翰·斯梅德利(John Smedley)对我们大喊大叫，说Twitch流媒体会有多大。”直到6月21日，一名前Twitch流媒体用户指控他性侵，Dariani一直担任在线表演者集团(Online Performers Group)的首席执行官，这是一家为Twitch流媒体用户提供全方位服务的管理公司。但八年前，当他被要求理解整个新的Twitch事件时，他发现“非常令人担忧”，他说。
Dariani说，他曾经与一家主要的多渠道网络合作，使用YouTuber营销一款游戏，后来才意识到，MCN拿走了他认为会流向YouTuber的一半资金-而不仅仅是10%或20%。他看到了创作者们是如何以新鲜的面孔和对来到这里感到兴奋的方式，例行公事地提出免费工作，以换取曝光。一位名为Cohh Carnage的流媒体用户现在有130万粉丝，他在SOE时代告诉Dariani，如果他能成为第一个流媒体即将推出的射手H1Z1的人，他会飞到索尼总部，并签名。达里亚尼犹豫不决。他说：“如果不付给他至少5万到10万美元来做这件事，那就太疯狂了。”“当时，这些人很多都很兴奋，你不用付钱给他们.”
在2015年PAX East期间，达里亚尼在波士顿威斯汀酒店宣布了OPG。该公司最终将管理数十个顶级流媒体，拥有15名员工。在推特上，达里亚尼公开反对令人不快的商业行为和该领域的性别歧视，将自己定位为正义的捍卫者。他向流媒体提供的合同避免了一些常见的欺诈行为，比如从粉丝的捐款中分一杯羹，将整个行业推向了可信度-尽管他确实从赞助和其他收入中收取了30%的费用。
但是，当达里亚尼还在组建一家以支持小人物而闻名的公司时，据称他自己也有不道德的行为。6月21日，一位前Twitch流媒体用户在Twitter上详细描述了与Dariani的一次经历，她称他在国有企业工作期间是“掠夺性的”。她声称，在一次游戏行业活动上见面和闲逛后，达里亚尼告诉她，这个行业“规模很小”，“以关系为基础”，然后提出了三人组的建议。达里亚尼没有回应连线对这些指控的置评请求，但他在Twitter上表示，虽然他不记得详细的事件，但他相信原告。
在Dariani被指控行为不当后，几位顶级流媒体人士离开了OPG。7月13日，OPG将关闭的消息传出。
与Twitch Talent合作的两名律师表示，绝大多数流媒体用户不会阅读或理解他们与管理公司或机构签署的合同。几位流媒体经理描述了新客户将如何在最初的交易中介绍主持他们Twitch频道的人作为代理。莫娜·易卜拉欣(Mona Ibrahim)表示：“我见过的几乎每一笔交易都带来了掠夺的可能性。”莫娜·易卜拉欣曾在IE法律集团工作，现在是Epic Games的律师。
丹·“Danotage”Herrera自2015年以来一直在Twitch上流媒体，拥有超过2.4万名粉丝，他受够了那些飘忽不定的业余爱好者提出的可口可乐赞助的前景。当一位新的潜在经理找到他时，他会和他在法学院的女朋友分享拟议中的合同。总是有非竞争条款，繁琐的排他性期限。“这些都是一份文件中的所有改动，实际上只是一份意向书，”埃雷拉说。“他们谈论这件事，就好像它具有法律约束力一样。”整件事把他描绘得太夸张了，所以他和OPG签约了。
易卜拉欣说，在很大程度上，有影响力的人不理解他们正在签署的合同。她说，有些人非常害怕显得麻烦，或者热切地认为自己没有谈判力，“更倾向于接受现状”。
拥有该平台令人垂涎的“合作伙伴”地位的Twitch Streamers通常授予那些平均同时收视率高达数百人、有定期播出时间表的人，他们可以自己赚钱；一线订阅费为每月5美元，他们通常会获得一半。他们还可以接受粉丝的捐款，并从中获得收入的一部分。经理的工作是在此基础上促进交易，通常收取15%至30%的分红。
然而，《连线》审查的合同差别很大。一份与YouTubers和Twitch Streamers合作的中型管理公司MoreHuang的一份合同规定，无论MoreHuang自己做什么，它都将获得全部毛收入的四分之一，这是Influencer为任何赞助商提供的服务的结果。如果MoreHuang真的发展了一项赞助协议，它将获得总费用的35%的补偿。“在我们与我们的人才的关系中，他们没有义务接受我们给他们带来的任何赞助，也没有义务接受我们给他们带来的任何赞助，”莫雷黄在一份声明中说。
一些合同要求拥有和营销飘带的形象或作品的权利；在MoreHuang的情况下，他们“由经理自行决定，永久地，并在整个宇宙中”。这意味着它可以永远在其营销材料中使用彩带的面孔或肖像，达到他们喜欢的任何目的。MoreHuang表示，它这样做是为了“制作材料，分享人才信息”。然而，易卜拉欣说，“没有人永远需要你的权利。”“这比管理合同中合理或必要的要繁重得多。”据两位定期审查ESPORTS专业人员及其团队之间的合同的消息人士称，这些条款在ESPORTS中也很常见。
其他组织模糊了管理和有影响力的营销之间的界限-当一个组织关注流媒体的福祉时，这是一种危险的修辞上的省略，而另一个组织则想从丰厚的交易中分得一杯羹。“假设你有一笔10万美元的交易，”Novo的首席营销官德文·纳什(Devin Nash)说。Novo是一家同时从事影响力管理和品牌推广的公司。“实际上，很多时候都会发生这样的事情：你签订有限合伙协议，然后给有影响力的人一份合同，比方说，5000美元或10000美元。你说，‘嘿，我给你搞到了一笔5000美元和10000美元的交易。’然后你把剩下的去掉，从5000美元到10000美元中抽取20%。“。
“因为有影响力的人从来不在谈话中，”纳什说，“他们不知道你得到了更多。”两位消息人士将这种做法描述为“二次浸泡”：尽可能多地从品牌中赚取资金，炒作彩带客户的触角，只对该机构的底线负责。
Ader的贾斯汀·沃登(Justin Warden)将其描述为一家“品牌第一的管理公司”，这意味着一个品牌可能会带着10万美元来到他身边，在他管理的三到四个有影响力的人之间进行分配。在Wire审查的一份Ader合同中，Streamer放弃了知道公司撮合的多影响者交易的总薪酬总额的权利，这意味着他们无法洞察自己是否获得了公平的百分比。ADER的协议规定，该公司为这些交易谈判单独的合同，“尽管它向Impencer提供了服务，但这是为了自己的最佳利益。”
贾斯汀·沃登说：“我们不能真诚地让有影响力的人看到我们业务的其余部分，因为我们的客户并不真的想让他们看到这一点。”他解释说，这是“严重违反保密协议”。他说，阿德经营着“微薄的利润率”。
他还表示，Ader不会对其代理部门安排的交易收取管理费，这样就不会向客户收取相同服务的两次费用。沃登表示，他实际上比该领域的其他公司更透明，这些公司将管理公司和营销公司分开。两家不同的公司可以收取两种不同的费用。
缺乏对金融交易的可见性可能会导致各种后果。三位深度参与该行业的消息人士称，他们的客户抱怨管理公司收取他们的捐款和认购收益的一部分，尽管没有直接帮助赚取这些收入。两位代表流媒体的消息人士表示，品牌向他们提供了私人激励措施，以说服多个客户公开推广他们的产品。
易卜拉欣指的是该行业的不透明性，他说：“以这种身份剥削个人是非常容易的。”“你可能是代表他们收钱，所以你控制着钱包。”在Kotaku最近的一篇文章中，纳什描述了一笔交易，在这笔交易中，一家管理公司重新启动了。
.
note_en:
Something about Novaruu looked like dollar signs from the moment she landed on Twitch. Blonde and with a radiant smile, Novaruu, then 19, had been gaming and hanging out with her growing fanbase for only a few weeks when she began receiving messages from entrepreneurial viewers offering to get her “deals”—vague promises to connect her with a capital-b Brand.
 One in particular stood out. He offered management tips—what game to play, how to play it—and said he’d get her free products to advertise on-stream. After a while, though, Novaruu discerned that this guy was, in her words, “suspicious.” His management tips mostly comprised telling her to play games and emulate other streamers he liked. And he didn’t bring her sponsorships—just affiliate links for product discounts, obtainable by anybody with a web browser and free space in their Twitch profile. He didn’t seem to have a background in this after all, which luckily also meant he never got her to sign a formal contract. Novaruu dropped him. A couple of months later, she would learn that, after changing his username, he had approached another streamer about managing them as well.
 Frustrated by the unprofessionalism, Novaruu took an interview with an established management company that dealt exclusively with pro gamers. These companies had cropped up in step with the booming industry of livestreaming games, which according to streaming software company Streamlabs is currently worth $50 billion. With her tens of thousands of followers, Novaruu decided it was time to formalize her hustle. Spire Management had everything: the posh website, a logo fit for an international airline, and an actual contract. Although Spire took 7 percent of the revenue she earned on Twitch and 15 percent of the sponsorships they secured for her, they made Novaruu feel like a pro. But after some early traction, the sponsorship deals dried up.
  “I was paying Spire $100 to $130 a month out of my own pocket and not even getting a brand deal every month,” says Novaruu. “I wasn’t comfortable with that.” (Spire did not respond to WIRED’s requests for comment.)
 Twitch has 6.2 million monthly broadcasters. Forty-five thousand of those have “partner” status, which means they can monetize their streams through donations and advertising. Only a couple hundred make a good full-time living off the platform, playing  Fortnite,  League of Legends, or  Call of Duty live while onlookers flood an adjacent chatroom with emotes. That hasn’t stopped countless thousands of aspiring pros from trying to reach that upper echelon.
  As rockstar streamers like Tyler “Ninja” Blevins pull in six- and seven-figure sponsorship deals and appear everywhere from  Ellen to Times Square billboards, the business of managing gaming luminaries has exploded. Over the past six years, dozens of agencies or management companies have cropped up specifically to service the microcelebrities of Twitch, Facebook Gaming, and YouTube’s gaming channels. Quality representation can mean lucrative deals with companies like G Fuel, Red Bull, Logitech, Lenovo, or even non-endemic brands like MAC or Postmates. It can provide support like sorting through streamers’ emails, sharing social media know-how, holding them accountable for deliverables, and helping book appearances at conventions.
  Yet the business of managing streamers is just as much of a Wild West as the broader industry. As traditional talent agencies like CAA, WME, and United Talent enter the scene, celebrity gamers are slowly realizing that the untamed environment they’re used to isn’t the norm.
 In interviews with two dozen people currently or formerly affiliated with the streaming management industry, many describe rampant exploitation they’ve seen or experienced. Sources detail management companies skimming extra money off the top of deals, acting as unlicensed agents, offering no exit clauses, and sometimes receiving offers of kickbacks behind the scenes. As noted where applicable, several sources WIRED interviewed for this story themselves later became subjects of allegations of sexual misconduct as part of gaming’s  #MeToo movement in June. WIRED also reviewed six contracts from some of the biggest players in the space to corroborate sources’ claims.
  “There are no industry standards that have been set or recognized by everyone yet. When there’s no standards of industry norms, people can try to do whatever they want,” says Scott Ball, president of End Game Talent and a former Twitch employee.
 Young, starry-eyed kids whose first-ever job is livestreaming video games on a platform that owes them nothing: Who could be an easier target?
 Managers first entered the games entertainment industry through YouTube. Once some early YouTubers attained microcelebrity status, multichannel networks like Maker Studios—founded in 2009 and acquired by Disney in 2014—and Machinima promised to help creators make a stable living off the ever-changing platform and its ever-changing policies. It didn’t take long for these businesses to sour; many early YouTube creators, reliant on immaterial “views” and unreliable ad sales, were promised more than they received. In 2017, Maker dropped support for nearly 60,000 YouTubers; many of them  felt relief. Stuck in endlessly auto-renewing contracts, and sometimes sacrificing 25 percent of their revenue, several YouTubers  complained that Maker cost more than it contributed—at least if you weren’t already a megastar.
  By 2011, the nascent streaming site Justin.tv had gained so much traction with gaming that it spun out Twitch to address that audience’s appetite for live content. Skilled and charismatic gamers now had their own platform to distinguish themselves as stars. As Twitch grew,  Starcraft II luminaries, hot girls who played  League of Legends,  Diablo III min-maxers, and  Call of Duty bros all grew audiences and a potential pathway to a career—or, at least, a side gig—playing video games. At the same time, esports pros, tournament organizers, and game publishers were gathering kindling for the conflagration that would be today’s reportedly billion-dollar esports industry. Together, the promises of Twitch streaming and esports would slowly normalize professional gaming—in the past, a pipe dream of children.
 Established management agencies were slow to turn to Twitch, a void filled mostly by amateurs and moderators who would promise unrealistic sponsorships or early access to low-budget indie games. A step up were friends of friends who had real connections at AAA developers or publishers.
 In 2015, while he was working on esports programming for the game studio Hi-Rez, Josh Belkin had a two-sided pitch: One of his friends was launching the game  Darkest Dungeon; a couple of his other friends streamed games on Twitch. “I texted buddies like, ‘Hey, can I help you market your game launch?’” Streamers gained both money and clout from the novelty of playing a new game. Developers benefited from the organic-ish influencer marketing. After working for free at first, Belkin began to ask for 15 percent of paid campaigns he brought to streamers.
  “It was just word of mouth,” Belkin says. “We were bringing some streamers paid activation because I had friends in industry who had budget to burn.” Belkin, now CEO of a company that develops software for streamers, counseled clients on business direction, their week’s content, how to grow the stream, and how to cross-pollinate audiences on social channels—stuff he only sort of, kind of knew how to do at the time.
 Other young and green fans of Twitch streamers were catching on too. When he was 22 years old and living on his friend’s couch, Justin Warden, now the CEO of management company and marketing agency Ader, began to wonder whether influencer marketing was a potentially lucrative workaround to the disruptive impact of ad blockers. Warden secured a sponsorship to attend the first Twitch Con, a large convention for Twitch streamers and their fans. On the back of a pink Walmart T-shirt, he wrote in Sharpie: “ASK US IF YOU WANT SPONSORSHIPS.”
  Warden had just dropped out of college and had no management background whatsoever. A year later, he spammed emotes in Byron “Reckful” Bernstein’s chat until he caught the popular streamer’s attention. In a video Bernstein made titled “Reckful accidentally helped a random viewer build a multi million dollar company,” he explained how before they even knew each other, Warden was just another viewer posting emotes in his chat. But one day Warden decided to email a subsidiary of the game company Cygames asking whether they’d pay Bernstein to play  Shadowverse. When they said yes, Warden cold-emailed Bernstein with the deal, saying he wanted a cut. It worked. Warden then used the same tactic on another streamer, and another. (Bernstein died in July.)
 The company he built out of those early attempts, Ader, now manages dozens of streamers and has 20 employees. In a now-removed 2018 Reddit  post, titled “How I took Ader from $0 to $8m yearly revenue in 2 years with no experience,” one of his bullet points included “over promise and over deliver.”
 Brandon Freytag, founder and senior vice president of talent at Loaded, a top management firm representing superstar streamers, was working in esports marketing in 2016 when he snagged his first clients over at Twitch: Saqib “LIRIK” Zahid and Jaryd “Summit1g” Lazar. They didn’t have available emails for incoming business, he says, so Freytag introduced himself with a message attached to a five-dollar donation. (Today, Zahid has 2.6 million followers and Lazar has 5.3 million. Loaded also manages Tyler “Ninja” Blevins.)
 In those early years, streamers consistently underestimated their own value. Without the business know-how to haggle, gaming content creators tended to lowball or ask for essentially random numbers, as did their friend-of-friend managers. Esports was particularly bad. When he was just getting started out, longtime  Call of Duty pro James "Clayster" Eubanks says his enthusiasm to be on a good team trumped his skepticism about contracts. (Teams share some of the same duties as managers.)
 “It’s an opportunity,” he says. “They’re not gonna give us a better deal. It was like they controlled everything. We just signed whatever was put in front of us.” When he got involved with agents, he says, it wasn’t much better. He says that the great majority of agencies he’s worked with in the esports space haven’t made him as much money as they’ve cost. “I’ve been with a couple agencies and they’re all asking for between 5 and 30 percent of gross,” he says. “They’ve brought me a couple brand deals that almost even it out. But in the end, I almost always ended up paying the agent more than they made me.”
  “It’s just not understanding leverage,” says Ben Nichol, a senior account executive at Next Generation Esports. “When you’re 20 and trying to negotiate your first six-figure contract for your buddy, on day three of that two-week waiting period you might call him back and say, ‘Fuck it, we’ll take it!’”
 Esports pros and Twitch streamers weren’t necessarily yoga-toned or professionally tanned, but AAA game publishers and developers had by the mid-aughts recognized gaming stars as influencers—real, relatable ones—for a demographic with a lot of expendable income. Managing Twitch streamers would soon formalize from a side-hustle into a job.
 “My first day at Sony Online Entertainment, my boss John Smedley was yelling at us about how big Twitch streaming would be,” says Omeed Dariani, who joined SOE in 2012. Until June 21, when a former Twitch streamer accused him of sexual predation, Dariani was the CEO of the Online Performers Group, a full-service management company for Twitch streamers. But eight years ago, when he was tasked with understanding that whole new Twitch thing, what he found was “very concerning,” he says.
  Dariani says he once worked with a major multi-channel network to market a game using a YouTuber, only to realize afterwards that the MCN had taken half of the money he thought would go to the YouTuber—not just 10 or 20 percent. He saw how creators, fresh faced and  just thrilled to be here, routinely offered to work for free in exchange for exposure. One streamer, who goes by Cohh Carnage and now has 1.3 million followers, told Dariani in the SOE days that he’d fly himself out to Sony headquarters and put himself up if he could be the first person to stream the upcoming shooter  H1Z1. Dariani balked. “It’d be insane not to pay him at least $50,000, $100,000 to do that,” he says. “At the time, a lot of these people were so excited, you didn’t have to pay them.”
 At a Boston Westin Hotel during 2015’s PAX East, Dariani announced OPG. The company would eventually manage dozens of top streamers with a staff of 15 employees. On Twitter, Dariani was known to speak out against unsavory business practices and sexism in the field, positioning himself as a champion for justice. The contracts he offered streamers avoided common grifts like skimming off from fans’ donations, nudging the industry toward credibility—although he did charge 30 percent from sponsorships and other income.
 But while Dariani was still formulating a company with a reputation for standing up for the little guy, he allegedly was engaged in unscrupulous behavior of his own. On June 21, a former Twitch streamer took to Twitter to detail an experience with Dariani that she describes as “ predatory” while he was still at SOE. She alleges that, after meeting and hanging out at a games industry event, Dariani told her that the industry was “small” and “relationship-based” before suggesting a threesome. Dariani did not respond to WIRED’s request for comment about these allegations, but  has said on Twitter that while he does not remember the events detailed, he believes his accuser.
 After Dariani was accused of misconduct, several top streamers left OPG. On July 13, news broke that OPG would close.
 Two lawyers who work with Twitch talent say that, overwhelmingly, streamers don’t read or understand the contracts they sign with management companies or agencies. Several streamer managers described how new clients will introduce the people who moderate their Twitch channels as agents in initial dealings. “Almost every deal I’ve seen poses the possibility of predation,” says Mona Ibrahim, a lawyer formerly with IE Law Group and now with Epic Games.
  Dan “Danotage” Herrera, who’s been streaming on Twitch since 2015 and has over 24,000 followers, was fed up with the flaky amateurs floating the prospect of Coca-Cola sponsorships. When a new potential manager approached him, he’d share the proposed contract with his girlfriend, who was in law school. There were invariably non-compete clauses, unwieldy exclusivity periods. “It was all changes in a document that was really just a letter of intent,” says Herrera. “They talked about it as if it was legally binding.” The whole thing sketched him out too much, so he signed up with OPG.
 Ibrahim says that, for the most part, influencers don’t understand the contracts they’re signing. Several are so afraid of appearing troublesome, or earnestly believe they have no negotiating power, and “are more inclined to accept it as is,” she says.
 Twitch streamers with the platform’s coveted “partner” status—generally granted to those with average concurrent viewership well into the hundreds and a regular broadcast schedule—can make money on their own; first-tier subscriptions cost five dollars a month, of which they typically get half. And they can receive donations from fans, from which they receive a share of revenue. The manager’s job is to facilitate deals on top of that, typically taking anywhere from 15 to 30 percent of the payout.
  Yet contracts reviewed by WIRED vary widely. One contract for the medium-sized management company MoreYellow, which works with both YouTubers and Twitch streamers, stipulates that it will receive a quarter of all gross income “as a result of services performed by Influencer for any Sponsor,” regardless of the work MoreYellow itself does. If MoreYellow actually develops a sponsorship deal, it will be compensated 35 percent of the total fee. “Within our relationship with our talent, they have no obligation to take on any sponsorships we bring them or that come to them," MoreYellow said in a statement.
  Some contracts ask for the right to own and market a streamer’s image or work; in MoreYellow’s case, at their “manager’s sole discretion, in perpetuity, and throughout the universe.” That means it can use the streamer’s face or likeness in its marketing materials forever, to whatever end they please. MoreYellow says it does so to “make materials and share information about talent.” However, “nobody needs your rights in perpetuity,” says Ibrahim. “That is far more onerous than is reasonable or necessary in a management contract.” According to two sources who regularly review contracts between esports pros and their teams, these clauses are common in esports as well.
 Other organizations blur the line between management and influencer marketing—a dangerous rhetorical elision when one looks out for the streamer’s wellbeing, and the other wants a cut of a fat deal. “Let's say you have a $100,000 deal,” says Devin Nash, CMO of Novo, a company that does both influencer management and branding. “What actually ends up happening a lot of the time is you do a limited partnership agreement and send the influencer a contract for, let’s say, $5,000 or $10,000. You say, ‘Hey, I got you a $5,000 and $10,000 deal.’ Then you take the rest off the top and take 20 percent of the $5,000 to $10,000.”
 “Because the influencer is never in the conversation,” Nash says, “they don’t know you got more.” Two sources described this practice as “double-dipping:” getting as much money out of the brand as possible, hyping up the streamer clients’ reach, and only acting accountable to the agency’s bottom line.
 Ader’s Justin Warden describes it as a “brand-first management company,” meaning that a brand might come to him with $100,000 to distribute between three or four influencers he manages. In one Ader contract reviewed by WIRED, the streamer waives the right to know the sum total compensation of a multi-influencer deal brokered by the company, meaning they have no insight into whether they received a fair percentage. Ader’s agreements specify that the company negotiates separate contracts for these deals “in its own best interest despite the services that it is providing influencer.”
  “We can’t in good faith give an influencer visibility into the rest of our business because our clients don’t really want them to have visibility into that,” says Justin Warden, explaining it’s a “big NDA violation.” He says Ader runs “razor-thin margins.”
 He also says that Ader does not collect a management fee for deals that its agency arm lines up, so as not to charge its clients twice for the same service. Warden says he’s actually being more transparent than other companies in the space that keep their management and marketing companies separate. Two different companies can collect two different fees.
 Lack of visibility into financial dealings can lead to a variety of consequences. Three sources deeply involved in the industry allege that their clients have complained of management firms that take a portion of their donations and subscription earnings despite not directly helping earn those. Two sources who represent streamers say brands have offered them private incentives to convince multiple clients to publicly promote their products.
 “It’s very easy to exploit individuals in that capacity,” says Ibrahim, referring to the industry’s opacity. “You may be receiving money on their behalf so you’re controlling the purse strings.” In a recent  Kotaku  article, Nash described a deal in which one management company recei

title: 对消息人士的采访和对游戏流媒体人才管理合同的审查表明，流媒体管理行业的剥削非常猖獗
title_en: Interviews with sources and a review of talent management contracts for game streamers show rampant exploitation in the streaming management industry

note:
印度的互联网人口现在是第二大，拥有超过5亿连接用户，本身就非常少。2011年初，中国的互联网用户不到1亿。
但Facebook最终在过去十年中扮演了关键角色。如此之多，以至于到了最后，这个社交巨头几乎接触到了这个国家的每一个互联网用户。根据移动洞察公司App Annie的数据，仅WhatsApp一项就覆盖了印度超过4亿互联网用户，超过了该国的任何其他应用。
Facebook在印度的这种影响力并没有被忽视。今天，这个国家的政客们严重依赖Facebook服务，包括WhatsApp，来传达他们的信息。但它也让事情变得复杂起来。
WhatsApp上流传着夺走生命的谣言，最近几周，印度两大政党的政客都指责该公司偏袒对方。
为了解决这些问题，以及Facebook希望在印度扮演的角色，该公司在印度的业务负责人阿吉特·莫汉(Ajit Mohan)加入了我们的Disrupt2020。以下是一些亮点。
“华尔街日报”(WSJ)最近的一篇报道称，Facebook在印度的高管之一安希·达斯(Ankhi Das)决定不从执政党的一名政治家那里撤职。报道称，她这么做是因为她担心这会损害该公司在印度的业务前景。
在争议爆发后莫汉的首次采访中，他驳斥了有关该国任何高管都有权影响Facebook如何执行其内容政策的说法。
“我们认为，对我们来说，保持开放、中立和无党派是很重要的，”他说。“我们深信不疑，我们的作用是作为一个中立的政党，允许各种言论，允许各种言论，包括政治表达，我们制定的许多指导方针都是为了确保我们真正实现表达和意见的多样性，只要我们能够确保人们的安全和保障得到保护。”
莫汉说，Facebook内部的内部流程和系统旨在确保一名员工或一群员工的任何意见和偏好“与公司和公司客观执行自己的政策完全分开”。
他说，个人可以对决策提供意见，但没有人-包括Ankhi Das-可以单方面影响Facebook在内容执法方面的决定。
“我们也允许在公司内部自由表达意见。我们对人们表达他们的观点没有任何限制，但我们认为这与我们内容政策的执行是分开的。[…]。在印度的背景下，内容政策本身是一个独立于安吉领导的公共政策团队的团队，“他补充说。
尽管Facebook在印度积累了数亿用户，但这个世界第二大市场对其利润贡献甚微。那么，为什么Facebook如此关心这个国家呢？
“印度正处于非常激动人心的经济和社会转型之中，数字在其中发挥着巨大的作用。仅在过去的四年里，就有超过5亿用户上网。这种转变的速度在人类历史上可能是前所未有的，甚至在世界各国正在发生的数字转型中也是如此。
他说：“对于像我们这样的公司来说，如果你看看WhatsApp和Instagram上的应用系列，你会发现我们在推动这种转变方面可以发挥有用的作用。”
莫汉表示，尽管Facebook从印度产生的收入并不多，但该公司已将自己确立为营销人员最信任的平台之一。他说：“在他们的营销议程中，他们把我们视为一个重要的合作伙伴。”
他说，该公司希望印度的广告作为GDP将会上升。“因此，随着时间的推移，广告收入将变得相当可观，”他说。
对于Facebook来说，印度也很关键，因为它允许该公司制造一些独特的产品，为印度解决问题，但可以在其他市场复制。该公司目前正在测试WhatsApp与新的印度电商JioMart的集成，以允许用户轻松跟踪他们的订单。WhatsApp尽管拥有超过20亿用户，但目前还没有商业模式。
他说：“我们认为有机会建立印度优先的模式，进行大规模试验，在我们取得成功的世界里，我们看到了将其中一些模式推向全球的巨大机会。”
Facebook通常不会投资初创企业。但在印度，该公司投资了社交商务公司Meesho，在线学习平台Unacademy-它甚至参与了后续融资-今年早些时候，它向Jio平台开了一张57亿美元的支票。那么，Facebook为什么要在印度采取这条投资路线呢？
“我们想创建一个项目，对早期初创企业进行少数股权投资，以弄清楚我们如何才能对初创企业创始人和整个生态系统有所帮助。起点是支持那些正在构建在某些方面是印度独有的、可以走向全球的模型的团队。自从我们在Meesho投资以来，他们在印尼进行了强有力的推动。这些都是我们认为可以增加价值的公司，也是我们可以从这些初创公司中学到的东西，“他说。
与Jio Platforms的合作遵循了不同的理论基础。“过去几年我们在印度谈论的转型，是Jio引发的，”他说。除此之外，Facebook正在探索与Jio合作的方式，比如与Jio的合资企业JioMart合作。他说：“这真的可以刺激中小企业，这对印度经济是有利的。”
莫汉表示，公司继续探索印度初创企业的更多机会，特别是那些团队认为Facebook可以增加价值的初创企业，但他表示，Facebook没有任何形式的授权必须投资，比如在三到四年内投资数十家初创企业。他说：“这不是一部量体剧。”
但这些公司，包括运营Jio平台和Reliance Retail的Reliance Industries，是否会获得Facebook服务的任何特殊访问权限。如果亚马逊、BigBasket、Grofers或Flipkart也想要与WhatsApp集成怎么办？莫汉说，Facebook平台对每家公司都是开放的，每个人都将获得相同水平的访问和机会。
在采访中，在印度运营迪士尼运营的Hotstar点播流媒体服务的莫汉还谈到了视频在印度的使用日益增长，WhatsApp Pay在印度推出的状况，Facebook如何看待印度对中国应用的禁令，等等。您可以在下面观看完整的采访。
note_en:
India’s internet population, now the second largest with more than 500 million connected users, itself was very small. In early 2011, the country had fewer than 100 million internet users.
 But  Facebook   ended up playing a crucial role in the last decade. So much so that by the end of it, the social juggernaut was reaching nearly every internet user in the country.  WhatsApp   alone reaches more than 400 million internet users in India, more than any other app in the country, according to mobile insight firm App Annie.
  This reach of Facebook in India didn’t go unnoticed. Politicians in the country today heavily rely on Facebook services, including WhatsApp, to get their message out. But it has also complicated things.
 Rumors have spread on WhatsApp that cost lives, and politicians from both the large political parties in India in recent weeks have accused the company of  showing favoritism to the other side.
 To address these issues, and the role Facebook wishes to play in India,  Ajit Mohan,   the head of the company’s business in the country, joined us at Disrupt 2020. Following are some of the highlights.
  A recent  report in WSJ claimed that Ankhi Das, one of Facebook’s top executives in India, decided against taking down a post from a politician from the ruling party. She did so, the report claimed, because she feared it could hurt the company’s business prospects in India.
 In Mohan’s first interview since the controversy broke, he refuted the claims that any executive in the country holds power to influence how Facebook enforces its content policy.
 “We believe that it’s important for us to be open and neutral and non-partisan,” he said. “We have deep belief and conviction that our enabling role is as a neutral party that allows speech of all kinds, that allows expression of all kinds, including political expression, and a lot of the guidelines that we have developed are to make sure that we really enable our diversity of expression and opinion so long as we’re able to make sure that the safety and security of people are protected.”
 Mohan said the internal processes and systems inside Facebook are designed to ensure that any opinion and preference of an employee or a group of employees is “quite separate from the company and the company’s objective enforcement of its own policies.”
 He said individuals can offer input on decisions, but nobody — including Ankhi Das — can unilaterally influence the decision Facebook takes on content enforcement.
 “We do allow free expression inside the company as well. We don’t have any constraints on people expressing their point of view, but we see that separate from the enforcement of our content policy. […] The content policy itself, in the context of India, is a team that stands separate from the public policy team that is led by Ankhi,” he added.
   Even as Facebook has amassed hundreds of millions of users in India, the world’s second largest market contributes little to its bottom line. So why does Facebook care so much about the country?
 “India is in the middle of a very exciting economic and social transformation where digital has a massive role to play. In just the last four years, more than 500 million users have come online. The pace of this transformation probably has no parallel in either human history or even in the digital transformation happening in countries around the world,” he said.
 “For a company like ours, if you look at the family of apps across WhatsApp and Instagram, we believe we have a useful role to play in fueling this transformation,” he said.
 Even as Facebook does not generate a lot of revenue from India, Mohan said the company has established itself as one of the most trusted platforms for marketers. “They look to us as a material partner in their marketing agenda,” he said.
 He said the company is hopeful that advertising as a GDP will go up in India. “Therefore ad-revenue will become substantial over time,” he said.
 For Facebook, India is also crucial because it allows the company to build some unique products that solve issues for India but could be replicated in other markets. The company is currently testing  an integration of WhatsApp, which currently does not have a business model despite having  over 2 billion users, with  new Indian e-commerce JioMart, to allow users to easily track their orders.
 “We think there is opportunity to build India-first models, experiment at scale, and in a world where we succeed, we see huge opportunity in taking some of these models global,” he said.
  Facebook does not usually invest in startups. But in India, the company has  invested in social-commerce firm Meesho,  online learning platform Unacademy — it even  participated in its follow-up round — and it wrote a  $5.7 billion check to Jio Platforms earlier this year. So why is Facebook taking this investment route in India?
 “We wanted to create a program for taking minority investments in early-stage startups to figure out how we could be helpful to startup founders and the ecosystem as a whole. The starting point was backing teams that were building models that in some ways were unique to India and could go global. Since we made an investment in  Meesho,   they have made a strong thrust in Indonesia. These are the kind of companies where we feel we can add value as well as we can learn from these startups,” he said.
 The partnership with Jio Platforms follows a different rationale. “The transformation we talked about in India in the last few years, Jio triggered it,” he said. Other than that, Facebook is exploring ways to work with Jio, such as with its partnership with Jio’s venture JioMart. “It can really fuel the small and medium business that is good for the Indian economy,” he said.
 Mohan said the company continues to explore more opportunities in Indian startups, especially with those where the teams think Facebook can add value, but he said there is no mandate of any kind that Facebook has to invest in, say dozens of startups in three to four years. “It’s not a volume play,” he said.
 But would these firms, including  Reliance Industries, which operates Jio Platforms and Reliance Retail, will receive any special access on Facebook’s services. What if Amazon, BigBasket, Grofers, or Flipkart want to integrate with WhatsApp, too? Mohan said Facebook platform is open for every firm and everyone will receive the same level of access and opportunities.
 In the interview, Mohan, who ran the Disney-run  Hotstar   on-demand streaming service in India, also talked about the growing usage of video in India, the state of WhatsApp Pay’s rollout in the country, what Facebook thinks of India’s ban on Chinese apps, and much more. You can watch the full interview below.
title: Facebook印度负责人阿吉特·莫汉(Ajit Mohan)就该公司最近在印度的政治争议、货币化机会和创业投资进行了问答
title_en: Q&A with Facebook India head Ajit Mohan on the company's recent political controversy in India, its monetization opportunities, and startup investments

15年来，GoPro一直是动作相机的行业标准。直到几年前，大疆(DJI)和Insta360等品牌才开始生产功能更多的动作相机，挑战了GoPro的领先地位。然而，今年，GoPro创造了迄今为止功能最强大的动作相机。
GoPro Hero 9几乎在所有方面都更加坚固：更大的电池，更大的机身，更高的视频和照片分辨率。此外，它第一次采用了没有塑料包装的可重复使用的外壳，这对地球、灰尘和空气都很重要，这让我有了探索的灵感。
是的，这台相机启发了我，从来没有阻止过我，以至于我希望在它面前有更多令人兴奋的事情要做。基于多年的动作相机知识，GoPro终于掌握了一种从未让我沮丧的硬件体验，同时坚持使用软件，尽管需要一些时间来适应，但拥有无穷无尽的分辨率和帧频功能。
不过，Hero9的价格更贵，如果你注册了GoPro Plus的一年订阅，价格是449.99美元或349.98美元，这包括在第一年的折扣价中，之后的价格是每年50美元。这比英雄8的起价高出50美元，比大疆的Osmo Action高出200美元，比双胞胎版Insta360 One R高出20美元。
第一件更大的事：它的实际大小。英雄9比去年的英雄8明显要大，厚一点、宽一点、高一点。不过，这并没有妨碍我想把它安装在哪里，也没有妨碍它随身携带的感觉，而且它仍然有同样大小的安装双叉，它们被磁性地存放在相机的底部。这意味着你不需要单独的外壳或外壳来连接配件，就像你在Hero7和更老的机型上所做的那样。
更大的尺寸带来了更大的背部触摸屏，对角线为2.27英寸。它的一侧有相同的USB-C端口、microSD卡插槽和电池盒，顶部有启动/停止按钮，侧面有电源和模式按钮。还有一面有一块奇怪的门闩，我以为可以拆下来，但事实并非如此。GoPro称之为“排水麦克风”，并解释说，它的设计是为了在相机被淹没后自动排水，这样当你浮出水面时，应该会有更清晰的音频。
技术是一种工具，因此我也是这样看待它的。在过去的两周里，我一直在测试Hero9，我和我自己的任何装备一样，都很努力，而且它没有任何划痕或划痕。
不过，如果镜头被刮伤，GoPro带回了Hero7的一个功能，这是我在Hero8上真正错过的：一个可拆卸的前镜头盖。在Hero 9上，你可以用力扭转并拉下位于镜头上方的盖子。这创造了一个更好的安装ND滤镜和镜头配件的方式，比如今年10月发布的Max Lens mod GoPro。不幸的是，没有向后兼容性，所以你的英雄7的ND过滤器不适合英雄9。
上一个重大的硬件变化，也是今年我最喜欢的新增功能，是前置屏幕。我会说80%的时间我使用的是GoPro，在取景时我面对着相机的前面。我总是迫不及待地在手机上打开GoPro的应用程序来查看我的相框，所以在相机前面即时查看是非常有用的。您可以更改前面屏幕的屏幕比例，显示什么信息，或者在Hero9的菜单中打开和关闭它。在录制或使用后见之明模式时，前面屏幕上会有一点滞后，这会保存相机在您按下录制按钮之前录制的前15秒或30秒的镜头，但除此之外，颜色会很鲜艳，信息也会很清晰。
更多的屏幕意味着更多的电量，所以GoPro将电池从Hero 8的1220mAh提高到了Hero 9的1720mAh。Hero 8以在正常使用时发热而闻名，但尽管电池更大，我并没有体验到Hero 9的触感变热。对于大量使用，仍然需要两节电池才能用上一整天。然而，在我非常寒冷的假期测试中，我在不使用的情况下关闭摄像头，只在晚上结束时观看回放，我能够用一块电池度过一整天，这与Hero 8不相上下。
下一个更重要的事情：决心。Hero9拥有几乎所有你可能需要的分辨率和帧频组合。2000万像素的传感器可以记录5K 30fps、4K 60fps，最高可达1080p 240fps。我大部分时间都是用默认的“GoPro”颜色设置用5K拍摄的。
5K的镜头非常清晰。我喜欢默认的颜色选项，它提高了饱和度，并保持了高对比度。将鲜艳的色彩与令人惊叹的相机内稳定性搭配起来，宾果-班戈，你就会有非常好看的镜头直接从摄像头里出来。另外，当我用4K编辑我们的评论视频时，我真的很享受有一点多余的空间来重新构图。
在企稳方面，今年还有更稳健的HyperSmooth 3.0。我经常对这种相机内的稳定性感到惊讶，今年的新功能是在线性帧拍摄时的相机内水平调平选项，这是从宽帧裁剪下来的。这使得在屏幕上保持地平线非常容易。
还提供了新的持续时间捕获、针对时间流逝的计划捕获模式和新的后见之明模式。启用后视模式时，电池耗电会更快一些，因为相机一直在记录。
2000万像素传感器拍摄的照片很不错。HDR照片确实需要很长时间来处理，我对HDR模式下阴影是如何提升到极致的印象并不太深。但在HDR关闭的情况下，照片会更有力、更明亮、更清晰。然而，在晚上，相机运行得有点慢，在照片中留下了模糊和纹理。GoPro的下一步将是提供天文摄影功能。我的要求是不是太高了？
最后但并非最不重要的一点是，这款相机有几个新的MOD面世。去年春天，GoPro发布了Hero8的Media Mod，它有一个内置的定向麦克风，HDMI输出，和一个冷鞋支架。今年10月，他们将发布一款售价99美元的Max Lens mod，这是该公司的第一款镜头mod。
最大镜头模式为Hero9带来了更宽的画面，并允许水平调平，无论相机的方向如何，因为它捕捉到的是传感器上的球形图像。不过，你只能用它拍到2.7k。我没有发现它比Hero 9的最宽设置宽太多，但我确实喜欢戴上它，不告诉Hero 9切换到Max Lens mod模式，这样我就可以捕捉到周围有一个奇怪的病态圆框的视频。看看上面的视频，看看它看起来是什么样子的样本。
所以，Hero9.GoPro恢复了可拆卸镜头，推出了MODS，安装了更大的电池，更大的传感器，5K视频，最重要的是，它终于有了一个有用的前屏。但对于所有这些重大改进，还有更大的代价。
然而，考虑到升级，449美元对于你得到的东西来说仍然是一个合理的价格。当然，你现在可以花350美元左右买到Hero8 Black，或者花250美元买大疆的Osmo Action，但这两款相机都不能以5K拍摄。还有一款Insta360的One R带有1英寸感应器MOD，售价500美元，但我更喜欢GoPro推出的更多颜色，而Hero9的价格要低50美元。
所有这些加在一起，英雄9是目前最大、最糟糕的动作相机-这款相机让我想要跟上它。
更正：这篇文章的早期版本说，要获得Hero9的折扣价，你必须购买GoPro服务的一年订阅。这是不正确的-第一年包括在购买价格中，之后是每年50美元。我们对这个错误感到抱歉。
note_en:
GoPro has been the industry standard for action cameras for over 15 years. It wasn’t until a few years ago that brands such as DJI and Insta360 began making action cameras with more features that challenged GoPro’s lead. This year, though, GoPro has created by far its most powerful action camera to date.
 The GoPro Hero 9 is more robust in almost every way: bigger battery, bigger body, bigger video and photo resolutions. Plus, for the first time, it comes in a reusable case with no plastic packaging, which is big for the Earth, dirt, and air this GoPro inspired me to explore.
 Yes, this camera inspired me and never stopped me, so much so that I wished I had more exciting things to do in front of it. Building upon years of action camera knowledge, GoPro has finally mastered a hardware experience that never frustrated me, while sticking to software that, although takes a bit of getting used to, holds endless resolution and frame rate capabilities.
  The Hero 9 is more expensive, though, at $449.99 or $349.98 when you sign up for a one-year subscription to GoPro Plus, which is included in the discounted price for the first year and is $50 per year after that. That’s $50 more than the Hero 8 started at, $200 more than DJI’s Osmo Action, and $20 more than the twin edition of the Insta360 One R.
    First bigger thing: its actual size. The Hero 9 is noticeably larger than last year’s Hero 8. A bit thicker, a bit wider, and a bit taller. This never got in the way of where I wanted to mount it or how it felt to carry around, though, and it still has the same sized dual prongs for mounting that are magnetically stowed on the bottom of the camera. That means that you don’t need a separate case or housing to attach accessories, like you did with the Hero 7 and older models.
 With the larger size comes a larger back touchscreen, 2.27 inches diagonally. It has the same USB-C port, microSD card slot, and battery compartment on one side, along with the start / stop button on the top and the power and mode button on the side. There is also this weird latch-looking piece on one side that I thought was removable, but it is not. GoPro calls this the “Drain Mic” and explained that it is designed to drain itself after the camera is submerged, which should make for clearer audio when you’re coming up out of water.
     Tech is a tool and therefore I treat it as such. In the two weeks I’ve been testing the Hero 9, I’ve been going as hard on it as any of my own gear and it’s held up with no scratches or dings.
 If the lens were to get scratched, though, GoPro brought back a feature from the Hero 7 that I truly missed on the Hero 8: a removable front lens cover. On the Hero 9, you can remove the cover that sits over the lens with a hard twist and pull. This creates a much better way of mounting ND filters and lens accessories, such as the Max Lens mod GoPro is releasing in October of this year. Unfortunately, there is no backwards compatibility, so your Hero 7’s ND filters won’t fit on the Hero 9.
   The last big hardware change, and my favorite addition this year, is the front-facing screen. I would say 80 percent of the time I’m using a GoPro, I am facing the front of the camera when framing the shot. I am always way too impatient to pull up GoPro’s app on my phone to see my framing, so an instant view on the front of the camera is very useful. You can change the screen ratio of the front screen, what information it displays, or toggle it on and off in the Hero 9’s menu. There is a bit of a lag on that front screen when recording or using Hindsight mode, which saves the previous 15 or 30 seconds of footage that the camera recorded before you hit the record button, but otherwise the colors are vivid and the information is clear.
   More screens mean more power usage, so GoPro bumped up the battery from 1,220mAh in the Hero 8 to a 1,720mAh in the Hero 9. The Hero 8 was known for heating up during normal use, but despite a bigger battery, I did not experience the Hero 9 getting hot to the touch. For heavy use, two batteries will still be needed to get through a full day. However, during my very chill vacation days of testing, where I was turning the camera off while not using it and only watching playback at the end of the night, I was able to get through a full day on one battery, which is right on par with the Hero 8.
 Next bigger thing: resolutions. There is just about every resolution and frame rate combo you could possibly need on the Hero 9. The 20-megapixel sensor can record 5K 30fps, 4K 60fps, and all the way up to 1080p 240fps. I spent most of my time filming in 5K with the default “GoPro” color setting on.
   The 5K footage is remarkably crisp. I dig the default color option that punches up the saturation and keeps a high level of contrast. Pair the vibrant colors with the amazing in-camera stabilization and, bingo-bango, you have really nice-looking footage straight out the cam. Also, when editing our review video in 4K, I really enjoyed having the bit of extra space for reframing.
 In terms of stabilization, there is also a more robust HyperSmooth 3.0 this year. I’m constantly amazed with this in-camera stabilization, and new this year is an in-camera horizontal leveling option when shooting in the linear frame, which is a bit cropped in from the wide frame. This makes it really easy to keep the horizon level on the screen.
 There is also a new duration capture, a schedule capture mode for time lapses, and the new Hindsight mode. The battery runs down a bit faster with Hindsight mode enabled because the camera is consistently recording.
 The photos from the 20-megapixel sensor are good. HDR photos do take a long time to process, and I was not overly impressed with how the shadows are brought up to extreme levels in HDR mode. But with HDR turned off, the photos are punchy, bright, and crisp. At night, the camera operates a bit slowly, though, leaving blur and grain in the photos. A great next step for GoPro would be an astrophotography feature. Am I asking too much?
  And last but not least, there are a few new mods coming out for this camera. Last spring, GoPro released the Media Mod for the Hero 8, with a built-in directional mic, HDMI outputs, and a cold-shoe mount. This October, they are releasing a Max Lens mod for $99, which is the company’s first lens mod.
 The Max Lens mod brings an even wider frame to the Hero 9 and allows for horizontal leveling no matter the orientation of the camera because of the spherical image it captures onto the sensor. You can only shoot up to 2.7K with it, though. I didn’t find it to be that much wider than the widest setting on the Hero 9, but I did enjoy putting it on and not telling the Hero 9 to switch to Max Lens mod mode so that I could capture video with a weird sick circle frame around it. Check out the video above for samples of what that looks like.
    So, the Hero 9. GoPro brought back the removable lens, is rolling out mods, put in a bigger battery, a larger sensor, 5K video, and most importantly, it finally has a useful front screen. But for all of these big improvements, there is that larger price tag.
 Considering the upgrades, though, $449 is still a reasonable price for what you’re getting. Sure, you can grab the Hero 8 Black for around $350 now or DJI’s Osmo Action for $250, but neither of those cameras can shoot at 5K. And then there is Insta360’s One R with a 1-inch sensor mod for $500, but I much prefer the color the GoPro puts out more, and well, the Hero 9 is $50 less.
 Add all that up and the Hero 9 is the biggest, baddest action camera out there — a camera that makes me want to keep up with it.
  Correction:  An earlier version of this article said that to get the discounted price on the Hero 9, you have to purchase a year subscription to GoPro’s service. That is incorrect — the first year is included with the purchase price, and it is $50 per year after that. We regret the error.
title: GoPro售价449美元的新Hero9：5K视频的评测清晰，是一个受欢迎的附加功能，前置屏幕很有用，而且坚固耐用，但比Hero8贵
title_en: Review of GoPro's new $449 Hero9: 5K video is crisp and a welcome addition, front-facing screen is useful, and it's rugged, but it's pricier than Hero8

VEEM已经为企业开发了一个全球支付网络，该公司周三宣布完成了一轮3100万美元的C系列融资。
这次加薪-使VEEM自2014年成立以来筹集的资金总额略高于1亿美元-吸引了我们的注意，原因有几个。
例如，总部位于北卡罗来纳州夏洛特的Truist Financial Corp.(由SunTrust和BB&amp；T合并而成)旗下的企业风险投资部门Truist Ventures牵头了这笔投资。此外，VEEM的其他支持者包括一个备受瞩目的集团，包括三菱UFG创新伙伴公司(MUFG Innovation Partners Co.)--日本最大的银行之一三菱UFG的CVC；AB Ventures--中东和非洲最大的银行之一阿拉伯银行的风险投资部门；以及现有的GV(前谷歌风险投资公司)、高盛(Goldman Sachs)、凯鹏华盈(Kleiner Perkins)、硅谷银行(Silicon Valley Bank)、澳大利亚国民银行风险投资公司(National Australia Bank Ventures)和趋势前进资本公司(Trend Forward Capital)。
这一轮也激起了我们的兴趣，该公司自称的使命是成为“企业的Venmo”，因为它通过其在线电汇服务帮助公司在国际上收发资金。B2B支付领域现在正在蓬勃发展，所以每当该领域的公司筹集新资金时，我们都会洗耳恭听。
特别是当其首席执行官马尔万·福兹利(Marwan Forzley)还创立了一家名为eBillme的初创公司，并将其出售给西联汇款(Western Union)时，这笔交易使西联汇款进入了在线支付领域。(在创立VEEM之前，Forzley还成为了电子商务的总经理，并与西联汇款建立了战略合作伙伴关系。)。
我与Forzley以及Truist Ventures的负责人凡妮莎·弗里兰(Vanessa Vreeland)进行了交谈，以了解更多关于加薪的情况，以及为什么加薪意义重大。根据Vreeland的说法，这笔投资标志着Truist自1月份成立以来首次支持一家公司。
她说：“我们想不出还有比这更好的公司来实现这一里程碑了。”VEEM的管理团队是一群鼓舞人心的创新者和远见卓识者，他们正在为中小企业解决一个关键的痛点。“。
VEEM发展迅速。虽然Forzley不愿透露任何具体的收入指标，但他确实分享了该公司历史上每年客户数量翻了一番的情况。目前，约有22.5万家企业使用VEEM的平台在100多个国家收发资金，该公司表示，该平台“将最好的传统系统与区块链技术和数字钱包可以提供的灵活性和速度结合在一起。”VEEM在其服务的每个国家和州都有许可证和监管，它支持50多种货币。
VEEM还与QuickBooks、Xero和NetSuite等其他平台集成，使企业主及其会计更容易“协作并建立全球网络”。
对于Truist来说，该公司的战略与它如何看待技术在塑造客户体验方面的作用非常一致。
弗里兰说：“他们专有的多轨技术使企业与他们的供应商、供应商和承包商之间能够通过一种易于使用、比传统的跨境B2B支付选择更具成本效益的服务连接起来--这是我们的客户需要的能力。”
就VEEM而言，它引以为豪的是简化了一个过程，一些人认为这个过程对发送者和接收者都是痛苦的，因为它需要大量时间，可能会花费相当多的费用，有时还需要亲自去银行参观。
“我们对提供类似于银行电汇的体验不感兴趣。Forzley告诉FinLedger：“通过重新思考资金在全球的流动方式，我们看到的是一种根本不同的体验。”“当你进行国际支付时，由于资金流动的动态，这是很复杂的。所以我们想要简化它，简化它，重新想象它。“。
例如，如果有人想要将资金转移到法国或中国，他们需要输入公司在法国的电子邮件地址和以欧元为单位的金额。弗兹利解释说，会有一条信息传给接收器：“嘿，你拿到钱了。”然后接收者选择他们想要接收的货币。
“这是一个非常大的市场，痛点是企业相当强烈的痛点，”他说。“如此多的人希望能够以一种简单的方式在世界各地转移资金。这是一个很长一段时间没有创新的市场。所以这有点太迟了。“。
VEEM的大量客户都是在线或电子商务企业。早期，公司主要使用VEEM向世界各地的供应商付款。根据Forzley的说法，一些人用它来支付劳动力，这是一个越来越受欢迎的用例。
Forzley说，VEEM将主要将新资本用于开发一个强大的渠道合作伙伴计划，这将扩大公司的地理足迹。
他补充说：“我们的渠道合作伙伴网络是我们更好地将我们提供的产品商业化和进一步扩大我们的市场开发努力的载体。”该公司还计划“进一步改进”和扩大其产品套装和功能。
换言之，根据Forzley的说法，VEEM在这轮融资中的关键目标是获得分销。
“如果你看看我们募集的资金类型，就会发现这不仅仅是钱的问题。它是金钱加价值，这里的价值在于我们可以在这个平台上获得进入更广泛的业务基础的机会和能力，“他告诉FinLedger。
至于Truist在帮助其增长计划方面的作用，Forzley指出，该银行以向小企业放贷而闻名，因此它可以获得“相当大的业务组合”。
对于Truist而言，弗理兰相信，该公司可以为VEEM及其未来投资组合的整体公司带来“大量优秀的行业专业知识”。
她告诉FinLedger：“我们真正关注的一件事是投资于与我们服务的客户一样多样化的投资组合公司。”
弗里兰回忆起几年前第一次见到Forzley的情景，当时Truist正在寻找帮助供应链融资的解决方案。
“我们的道路分道扬镳了一段时间，在他寻求提高他的C系列赛时，我们重新建立了联系，”她说。“看到他制造的产品真的很令人兴奋，你知道，最值得注意的是，他可以在五条不同的轨道上汇款，其中一条轨道是一种加密货币，允许以非常经济高效的方式进行实时外汇结算。能够向我们的中小型企业客户提供这种类型的解决方案是我们一直希望做的事情，所以我很高兴他能制造出如此强大的产品，并对平台上已有的客户有如此大的吸引力。“。
弗里兰还证实，未来将有机会(一旦SunTrust和BB&Amp；T的内部系统整合完成)获得VEEM的“完全商业化”。
“我们希望在未来12个月内启动并运行一个试点项目，”她说。
note_en:
Veem, which has developed a global payments network for businesses, announced Wednesday the closing of a $31 million Series C round.
 This raise – which brings Veem’s total raised since its 2014 inception to just over $100 million – caught our attention for a few reasons.
 For one,  Truist Ventures – the corporate venture capital arm of Charlotte, N.C.-based  Truist Financial Corp. (which was formed from the  merger of  SunTrust and  BB&T) – led the investment. Plus, Veem’s other backers include a high profile group including  MUFG Innovation Partners Co. – the CVC of  MUFG, one of the largest banks in Japan;  AB Ventures – the venture arm of  Arab Bank, one of the largest banks in the Middle East and Africa; as well as existing  GV (formerly Google Ventures),  Goldman Sachs,  Kleiner Perkins,  Silicon Valley Bank,  National Australia Bank Ventures and  Trend Forward Capital.
 What also piqued our interest about this round was the company’s self-described mission to become the “Venmo for businesses,” as it helps companies send and receive money internationally with its online wire-transfer service. The B2B payments space  is booming right now, so any time a company in the space raises fresh capital, we’re all ears.
  Especially when its CEO, Marwan Forzley, also founded and sold a startup called  eBillme to  Western Union in a deal that gave the latter company entry into the world of online payments. (Forzley also went on to become the GM of eCommerce and strategic partnerships with Western Union before founding Veem).
 I talked both with Forzley as well as Vanessa Vreeland, head of  Truist Ventures, to hear more about the raise and why it’s significant. The investment marks Truist’s first time backing a company since its formation in January, according to Vreeland.
 “We can’t imagine a better company to hit this milestone with,” she said. “Veem’s management team is an inspiring group of innovators and visionaries that are solving a critical pain point for small- and medium-sized businesses.”
  Veem is growing rapidly. While Forzley would not disclose any specific revenue metrics, he did share that the company has historically been doubling its number of customers on a yearly basis. Currently, about 225,000 businesses send and receive money in more than 100 countries using Veem’s platform, which the company says “combines the best of traditional systems with the flexibility and speed that blockchain technology and digital wallets can provide.” Veem is licensed and regulated in each country and state it serves, and it supports over 50 currencies.
 Veem also integrates with other platforms such as QuickBooks, Xero and NetSuite to make it easier for business owners and their accountants “to collaborate and build a global network.”
 For Truist, the company’s strategy aligns well with how it views the role of technology in shaping the client experience.
 “Their proprietary multi-rail technology enables connections between businesses and their vendors, suppliers and contractors through a service that is easy to use and more cost effective than legacy cross-border B2B payment options – capabilities that our clients need,” Vreeland said.
 For its part, Veem prides itself on simplifying a process that some view as painful for both the sender and receiver in that it takes a lot of time, can cost quite a bit in fees and sometimes requires an in-person bank visit.
 “We’re not interested in providing an experience that’s similar to a bank wire. We’re looking at a fundamentally different experience by rethinking the way money should move globally,” Forzley told FinLedger. “When you do international payments, it’s complex because of the dynamics of how money moves. And so we wanted to dumb it down, simplify and reimagine it.”
 If someone wants to move money to France or China for example, they enter the email address of the company in France and the amount of money in euro units. A message goes out to the receiver that ‘hey you’re getting paid,’ Forzley explained. And then the receiver then chooses the currency they want to receive it in.
 “This is a very large market and the pain point is a fairly strong pain point for businesses,” he said. “So many want to ability to move money around the world in a simple way. And it’s a market that has not seen innovation for a long period of time. So it’s kind of overdue.”
 A large number of Veem’s customers are online, or ecommerce, businesses. Early on, companies primarily used Veem to pay suppliers around the world. Some use it to pay labor, an increasingly popular use case, according to Forzley.
  Veem will use the new capital primarily toward the development of a robust channel partner program that will widen the company’s geographic footprint, Forzley said.
 “Our channel partner network serves as our vehicle to better commercialize our product offering and further expand upon our market development efforts,” he added. The company also plans to “further improve” and expand its product suite and capabilities.
 In other words, Veem’s key objective with the round is to get distribution, according to Forzley.
 “If you look at the type of money we raised, it’s not just money. It’s money plus value and the value out here is access and the ability to get into a broader base of businesses that we can have on the platform,” he told FinLedger.
  As for Truist’s role in helping in its growth plans, Forzley points out that the bank is known for lending to small business so it has access to “fairly large portfolios of business.”
 For Truist’s part, Vreeland believes the firm can bring “a lot of great industry expertise” to Veem and its future portfolio companies in general.
 “And one of the things that we’re really focused on is investing in portfolio companies that are as diverse as the customers that we serve,” she told FinLedger.
 Vreeland recalls first meeting Forzley several years ago when Truist was looking for a solution to help with supply chain finance.
 “Our paths diverged for a period of time and we reconnected as he was looking to raise his Series C,” she said. “It was really exciting to see the product that he has built, you know, most notably the fact that he can send money over five different rails and one of those rails is a cryptocurrency that allows for real time foreign exchange settlement in a very cost effective way. Being able to offer that type of solution to our small- and medium- sized business clients is something that we had been looking to do and so I was excited that he had built such a robust product, and has so much traction with customers already on the platform.”
 Vreeland also confirmed there will be opportunity down the road (once Suntrust and BB&T’s internal systems are integrated) to get “a full commercialization” with Veem.
 “We’re looking to get a pilot up and running within the next 12 months,” she said.
title: 由Truist Ventures牵头的全球企业支付网络VEEM筹集了3100万美元的C系列资金，使其筹资总额达到1亿美元以上
title_en: Veem, a global payments network for businesses, raises $31M Series C led by Truist Ventures, bringing its total raised to $100M+

“我们将不遗余力地在OECD和G20框架内达成协议。但毋庸置疑的是：如果协议达不到提供长期可持续收入的公平税制，欧洲将在明年初拿出一份提案，“她对欧洲议会议员表示.。
在这场内容广泛的演讲中-冯德莱恩还呼吁到2020年成为欧洲的“数字十年”-冯德莱恩承诺，欧盟将把今年早些时候宣布的7500亿欧元冠状病毒支持基金的五分之一(1500亿欧元)用于数字投资。
她说：“现在是投资欧洲科技公司的最佳时机，从索非亚到里斯本，再到卡托维兹，各地都在发展新的数字中心。”他说：“作为一个联盟，我们拥有取得成功的人才、理念和力量。这也是我们将向NextGenerationEU投资20%用于数字业务的原因。“。
“我们用类比的方式可以做的事情已经到了极限。而这种巨大的加速才刚刚开始。我们必须使今年成为欧洲的数字十年，“冯德莱恩补充说。
“我们需要一个数字欧洲的共同计划，明确2030年的目标，比如互联互通、技能和数字公共服务。我们需要遵循明确的原则：隐私权和连接权、言论自由、数据自由流动和网络安全。
“但欧洲现在必须在数字化方面发挥带头作用，否则它将不得不效仿为我们设定这些标准的其他国家。”这就是我们必须迅速行动的原因。“。
在激动人心的“数字主权”言论之下，这场演讲在科技政策方面并没有提供太多新的东西-但欧盟主席证实，明年将对欧洲的竞争规则和使用人工智能的监管进行更新。
欧盟委员会目前正在就是否需要一个新的竞争工具来应对可能导致小费市场的数字网络效应，以及更广泛地围绕即将出台的数字服务法案(演讲中没有直接提到该法案)进行咨询。
“在个性化数据方面--企业对消费者--欧洲一直太慢，现在依赖于其他国家，”她说。“工业数据不能出现这种情况。在这方面，好消息是欧洲处于领先地位--我们有技术，关键是我们有这个行业。“。
“我们在三月份提出了我们的新行业战略，以确保行业能够引领绿色和数字的双重过渡。过去的六个月只是加速了这一转变-在全球竞争格局正在发生根本性变化的时候。正因如此，我们会在明年上半年更新我们的行业策略，并调整我们的竞争架构，而我们的竞争架构亦应与时并进。“。
她强调，数字投资的优先事项是建立欧洲云的计划-这将基于Gaiax联邦数据基础设施，该基础设施正在开发泛欧盟数据共享的共同需求。(这是欧盟委员会今年早些时候宣布的围绕工业数据重用的重大努力的一部分。)。
第二个被点名的投资重点领域是人工智能-欧盟主席提到了这项技术在实现“农业精准农业、更准确的医疗诊断和安全的自动驾驶”等创新方面的潜力。然而，她也强调了制定规则来包裹这项技术的重要性，重申了欧盟立法者的信念，即需要一个框架来确保他们所说的“以人为中心”的人工智能。
今年早些时候，欧盟发布了一份白皮书，提出了监管人工智能“高风险”应用的建议。尽管提案的最终形状要等到2021年。
冯德莱恩还建议，立法者正在寻找方法，让消费者在大数据驱动的人工智能时代更多地控制他们的数据是如何使用的。
“我们想要一套以人为中心的规则。算法不能是一个黑匣子，如果出了问题，必须有明确的规则。委员会将於明年提出有关法例。“。
他说：“这包括对我们个人数据的控制，而今天(我们)仍然很少掌握这些数据。每当一个App或网站要求我们创建新的数字身份或通过大平台轻松登录时，我们都不知道我们的数据在现实中会发生什么。“
为此，她表示，欧盟委员会希望开发一种“安全的欧洲电子身份”，欧盟公民可以在欧盟任何地方使用--“做任何事情，从纳税到租自行车”。她补充说，这将是“一种我们可以控制自己哪些数据以及如何使用数据的技术”，并重复了她的数字主权主题。
欧盟委员会正在审查围绕开斋节的现有法规，包括进行定于下个月结束的咨询-它表示正在研究采用开斋节和可信服务的障碍，并考虑如何将框架演变为“欧盟数字身份”。
现在听起来立法者似乎有了全面改革开斋节的具体计划-目的是促进一种专有的数字身份验证机制，该机制可以帮助推动围绕数字化和数据重用的更广泛的战略。
“新冠肺炎纾困”数字支出的第三个重点是基础设施，计划围绕宽带接入推进。
“通过NextGenerationEU促进投资是一个独特的机会，可以推动(宽带)扩展到每个村庄。这就是为什么我们希望将我们的投资重点放在安全连接上，专注于5G、6G和光纤的扩展，“冯德莱恩说，并补充说：”NextGenerationEU也是一个独特的机会，可以开发更连贯的欧洲连接和数字基础设施部署方法。“。
她的演讲还强调了一项80亿欧元的计划投资，用于开发下一代超级计算机。并再次呼吁欧洲工业界开发自己的下一代芯片-“这将使我们能够高效和安全地使用不断增长的数据量”。
“这一切本身都不是目的--这事关欧洲的数字主权，无论规模大小，”她补充道。
冯德莱恩还在环境和气候变化带来的风险上花费了相当多的时间。
欧盟主席透露，欧盟委员会提议将2030年减排目标从40%提高到55%。
与数字项目相比，欧洲绿色协议在新冠肺炎救济支出中所占的比例将更大-尽管据推测，可能会有一些重叠，冯德莱恩谈到“一个我们使用数字技术建设更健康、更绿色社会的世界”。
她说，下一代欧盟基金的37%(2770亿欧元)将直接用于绿色交易目标。
这笔支出似乎将通过对充电基础设施的投资，对电动汽车产生重大推动作用。她提到的其他重点领域是氢气取代煤炭用于工业生产；以及调整建筑业，使其更可持续、污染更少，包括通过使用人工智能和智能技术。
她说：“NextGenerationEU应该投资于影响最大的欧洲灯塔项目：氢气、翻新和100万个充电点。”“我希望NextGenerationEU创建新的欧洲氢谷，以实现我们的工业现代化，为我们的汽车提供动力，并为农村地区带来新的生活。”
“我们的建筑物产生了我们40%的排放量。他们需要变得更少浪费、更便宜、更可持续。“她补充说。“我们知道，如果应用木材等有机建材和人工智能等智能技术，建筑业甚至可以从碳源变成碳汇。”
支持大规模转向循环经济所需的系统变革被称为“欧洲的新文化项目”。
“每一个动作都有自己的观感。我们需要赋予我们的系统性变革自己独特的美学-将风格与可持续性相匹配，“她说，并宣布了一项计划，将建立”一个新的欧洲包豪斯“-也就是”一个建筑师、艺术家、学生、工程师和设计师共同努力实现这一目标的共同创作空间“。
